---
title: "Identifying March Madness Upsets with BOSS"
author: "Sam Hamelsky and Ananya Das"
format: pdf
---

## 

## Introduction 

Each year, the NCAA Men’s Basketball Tournament attracts millions of fans who try to predict the outcome of the bracket. First round upsets remain one of the hardest parts of the tournament to predict and frequently are the cause of incorrect brackets. Only a small number of tournament games are played each season and the event is single elimination making upset predictions a difficult statistical problem. 

This project examines a framework introduced in the paper Identifying March Madness Upsets with BOSS. Rather than directly predicting winners, the authors treat upset prediction as a similarity problem. Their method uses Balance Optimization Subset Selection (BOSS), a framework originally developed for causal inference, to identify current tournament matchups that most closely resemble historical upset games.

Our goal is to replicate and extend the original study using NCAA tournament data through 2026. In addition to reproducing the core BOSS framework, we evaluate how the model performs when expanded to include 16-seed matchups.

## Original Paper 

The original paper: <https://www.degruyterbrill.com/document/doi/10.1515/jqas-2016-0062/html>

The original paper proposed a new way to forecast NCAA tournament upsets among first round games involving 13, 14, and 15 seeds. It applied this method to data ranging from 2000-2015, but the paper did not include 16 seeds given that when it was published in 2017 there had been no 16 seeds upsets.  Instead of directly modeling game outcomes, the authors first identified historical tournament games in which lower seeded teams defeated higher seeded opponents. These upset games were treated as a reference group.

Current-year tournament matchups were then compared to this historical upset group using team performance statistics. The goal was to determine which current games had statistical profiles most similar to past upsets. Games selected most frequently across repeated model runs were identified as the most likely upset candidates.

The paper reported strong results over the period from 2000 through 2015. Out of 26 predicted upsets, 10 were correct, corresponding to an accuracy rate of 38.4%. This outperformed simple random selection strategies and suggested that historical upset patterns may contain predictive value.

## Data

To replicate the original study, we created a dataset combining regular season team statistics with NCAA tournament outcomes. Regular season team performance statistics were collected from TeamRankings.com for seasons spanning 2000 through 2026. This data was gathered using the original web scraper provided with the paper, which we expanded to include additional seasons and take into account any changes to the original website. The dataset contained 115 variables describing offensive efficiency, defensive efficiency, shooting performance, rebounding, turnovers, tempo, strength of schedule, and other team characteristics. Statistics were collected only through Selection Sunday each season, meaning they reflected regular season performance and did not include tournament results.

Tournament outcomes were collected separately for all Round of 64 games. For each season, matchups were restricted to first round contests involving seeds 1 through 16. Both regular season statistics and tournament outcomes were stored in CSV files for processing.

Because team naming conventions differed across sources, team names were standardized using automated mappings and manual corrections. This ensured that regular season statistics aligned correctly with tournament participants.

For each matchup, the lower seeded team and higher seeded team were identified numerically. An upset was defined as any game in which the lower seeded team defeated the higher seeded team.

The original paper compares teams using ranking differences rather than raw statistics, and we followed the same approach. The reason we use ranking differences is it can be difficult to compare raw statistics across years as rules and the play of the game can greatly change. Within each season, every team was ranked for each of the 115 statistics. For a given statistic, a rank of 1 indicated the strongest team in that category, while the lowest rank (typically around 346, depending on the number of Division I teams that season) indicated the weakest. Ties were handled by assigning the average of the tied ranks. For variables where higher values are better, ranks were assigned in descending order. For variables where lower values are preferable, ranks were assigned in ascending order.

For each season, the ranked regular season statistics were first merged with the Round of 64 tournament bracket data so that every first round matchup contained the rankings of both the lower seeded team and the higher seeded team across all 115 variables. This allowed us to directly compare the two teams within each game.

For each tournament matchup, a matchup statistic was then calculated as:

**Matchup Difference = Rank of Lower Seed − Rank of Higher Seed**

This transformation standardizes variables across seasons and places all statistics on a comparable scale. Positive values indicate that the favored team performed better in that category, while negative values indicate that the underdog performed better.

After computing these ranking differences for every variable, the final dataset contained one row per tournament game and included all 115 engineered matchup statistics for contests between 1 vs 16, 2 vs 15, 3 vs 14, and 4 vs 13 seeds across all seasons in our sample.
